mnemonics.title: Six Categories of System Calls
mnemonics.content: P-FID-CP: Process Control, File management, Device management, Information maintenance, Communication, Protection - 'Please Find Delicious Cheese Pizza'

mnemonics.title: Standard File Descriptors
mnemonics.content: 0-1-2: Standard Input (0) reads, Standard Output (1) writes results, Standard Error (2) writes errors - '0I 1O 2E' or '012 I O E'

mnemonics.title: fork()-exec() Pattern
mnemonics.content: fork-CREATE, exec-REPLACE: 'fork makes a copy, exec swaps the program' - like photocopying a document then writing new content on it

acronyms.usage: Unique number assigned to each process, returned by fork() and used by wait() to track child processes
Mechanisms like pipes, message queues, shared memory for processes to communicate
Flag used in open() system call to open a file for both reading and writing
Signal number 9 used to forcefully terminate a process; cannot be caught or ignored
Bitmask that determines default permissions for newly created files (e.g., 022 gives 755)
Non-negative integer used to identify an open file in a process's file descriptor table

visualTips.visualization: Think of user mode as the 'living room' and kernel mode as the 'server room'. System calls are like requests that must go through a 'receptionist' (kernel) to access privileged operations. Draw two circles: outer ring (user mode), inner circle (kernel), with a bridge labeled 'system call interface'.
Draw one process box splitting into two identical boxes. Label parent 'fork() returns child PID' and child 'fork() returns 0'. Show arrows indicating execution continues after the split.

Draw a table with columns: Index (0,1,2,3...), File Descriptor, Points To. Show stdin/stdout/stderr pointing to terminal, other entries pointing to files. This visualizes how file descriptors reference open files.
Draw process lifecycle: Running → Terminated (Zombie) → Removed. Show parent calling wait() removing the zombie state. Use a ghost icon for zombie state.

analogies.analogy: System calls are like ordering food in a restaurant. You (user program) cannot enter the kitchen (kernel) directly. You place an order (system call), the chef (kernel) prepares it, and you receive the result (return value). You never directly access the kitchen.

fork() is like cell division - one cell (parent process) splits into two. Both have the same genetic material (code/data) initially, but one can change independently. The parent gets the child's ID, like knowing which new cell was created.

analogies.concept: File Descriptor as Restaurant Table Number
analogies.analogy: When you open a file, the kernel assigns it a table number (file descriptor). You don't need to remember the file details; just use the table number (fd) to place orders (read/write) and when done, close the table.

exec() is like a software update on your phone - the same phone (process) but with a completely new operating system/program. The process ID stays the same, but the program code is completely replaced.
